nl: Find all recordings in MusicBrainz by Miles Davis
gold_sparql:
PREFIX rdf:  <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX wd:   <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX wdt:  <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
PREFIX mb:   <https://linkedmusic.ca/graphs/musicbrainz/>
SELECT ?recording
WHERE {
  GRAPH mb: {
    ?artist a mb:Artist .
    ?artist wdt:P2888 wd:Q93341 .
    ?recording a mb:Recording .
    ?recording wdt:P175 ?artist .
  }
}
LIMIT 100
notes: Migrated from dev_generated.json (dev-002). Q93341 = Miles Davis. Same pattern as Taylor Swift train example but different artist.

nl: How many recordings are there in MusicBrainz for each genre?
gold_sparql:
PREFIX rdf:  <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX wdt:  <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
PREFIX mb:   <https://linkedmusic.ca/graphs/musicbrainz/>
SELECT ?genre (COUNT(?recording) AS ?recordingCount)
WHERE {
  GRAPH mb: {
    ?recording a mb:Recording .
    ?recording wdt:P136 ?genre .
  }
}
GROUP BY ?genre
ORDER BY DESC(?recordingCount)
LIMIT 100
notes: Migrated from dev_generated.json (dev-005). Aggregation with GROUP BY and ORDER BY. Large result set — compare live.

nl: What are the 5 feast days with the most chants in Cantus DB, and what event or person are they named after according to Wikidata? Return the feast label in English and the named after label in English.
notes: Inner subquery counts chants per Wikidata-linked feast (FILTER(isURI(?feast)) selects only the 588 feast QID rows, ignoring the majority of string-literal feast values), takes the top 5 by count. Only 5 QIDs are sent to Wikidata SERVICE. Wikidata returns the English rdfs:label for each feast (?feastLabel) and OPTIONAL P138 (named after) with English label (?namedAfterLabel). Results: Christmas (7,376 chants, no P138), Epiphany (5,787, no P138), Candlemas (5,336, named after: Presentation of Jesus at the Temple), Feast of the Assumption of Mary (5,241, named after: Assumption of Mary), Feast of the Ascension (5,092, named after: Ascension of Jesus). Musicologically: the five most-chanted feasts in the Western liturgical calendar — all central Christological or Marian observances. Christmas and Epiphany lack P138 in Wikidata because they are self-definitional feasts, not 'named after' any discrete event node. Distinct from gt-075 (same top-N pattern for Cantus Index with labels only) in database (CantusDB, 3.6M triples) and Wikidata enrichment depth (adds 2-hop OPTIONAL P138). Tests: COUNT + GROUP BY + ORDER BY + LIMIT before SERVICE (to control QIDs sent), OPTIONAL 2-hop inside SERVICE, mixed isURI guard for heterogeneous property values.

nl: What are the top 5 feast days with the most chants in Cantus Index?

LIMIT 5 in subquery passes only 5 URIs to SERVICE, avoiding timeout. Results: Christmas (857), Epiphany (544), Feast of Saint Mary Magdalene (532), Feast of the Ascension (435), Feast of Saint Ursula (388). Tests: isURI filter, top-N before SERVICE.

nl: Return the birth places of the 10 most prolific jazz soloists in Dig That Lick.
notes: dtl:Solo.wdt:P175 stores performer as a Wikidata QID URI directly. Inner subquery counts all solos per performer, orders by count descending, and takes the top 10 — only 10 QIDs are passed to SERVICE. Wikidata returns the English rdfs:label for each performer and, via OPTIONAL, their birth city (wdt:P19) with English label. 13 rows returned (not 10) because some performers have multiple P19 values in Wikidata: Sonny Rollins has both 'Harlem' and 'New York City' (Q60) as birth places; Charlie Parker appears twice (two Kansas City P19 entries with different QIDs, one for Kansas City Missouri and one for the broader metro); Lester Young has two Woodville entries. Top 10 soloists by solo count: Joshua Redman (49, Berkeley CA), Sonny Rollins (43, Harlem/NYC), Dexter Gordon (41, Los Angeles), Art Pepper (37, Gardena CA), Bob Cooper (34, Pittsburgh), Charlie Parker (29, Kansas City), Lester Young (24, Woodville MS), Dizzy Gillespie (21, Cheraw SC), Lionel Hampton (19, Louisville KY), Sonny Stitt (16, Boston MA). Musicologically: the most-analysed soloists in the WJazzD/DTL corpus skew heavily toward bebop and cool jazz, with almost all performers being from the American South or Mid-Atlantic. New federated pattern for DTL: top-N aggregation before SERVICE with OPTIONAL multi-valued property enrichment — distinct from the existing DTL federated query (performer gender, CSV C3) in using performer birth geography rather than demographic filtering. Tests: GROUP BY + COUNT + ORDER BY + LIMIT inside subquery to cap QIDs, OPTIONAL 2-hop inside SERVICE.

nl: What countries are the 10 most documented composers in RISM associated with according to Wikidata?
notes: Inner subquery aggregates RISM sources per composer (rism:Person.wdt:P2888 links to Wikidata QID) and takes the top 10 by source count, capping the QIDs sent to SERVICE at 10. Wikidata returns the composer's English label and OPTIONAL citizenship (wdt:P27). 16 rows returned (not 10) because some composers have multiple P27 values: Handel (3 citizenships: Holy Roman Empire, Brandenburg-Prussia, Kingdom of Great Britain), Bach (2), Chopin (4). Top 10 composers: Joseph Haydn (17,179 sources), Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (17,155), George Frideric Handel (12,160), Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina (10,615), Michael Haydn (9,313), Johann Sebastian Bach (9,150), Ignaz Pleyel (8,588), Frédéric Chopin (6,835), Johann Adolph Hasse (6,446), Georg Philipp Telemann (5,848). All are European classical/Baroque composers, confirming the core of the RISM manuscript tradition. Follows the top-N-then-federate pattern of gt-073. Tests: subquery with GROUP BY + LIMIT before SERVICE, OPTIONAL inside SERVICE for multi-valued properties.

nl: Find works in the SIMSSA digital scores library whose composers were born in Italy.
notes: simssa:Work.wdt:P86 links to a simssa:Person node; simssa:Person.wdt:P2888 provides the Wikidata QID. The inner subquery passes all distinct (work, workLabel, composerQID) triples to SERVICE (only ~10 distinct composer QIDs in SIMSSA, making the call tractable). Wikidata resolves wdt:P19 (place of birth) → ?birthplace, then checks wdt:P17 (country) = wd:Q38 (Italy). 12 distinct works returned: Missa Aeterna Christi munera, Missa Hodie Christus Natus Est, Missa Papae Marcelli, Missa Te Deum laudamus (Palestrina), plus eight Italian madrigals (Amor che mi tormenti, Ben mi credea passar mio tempo homai, Chi della fede altrui piglia sospecto, De perché in odio m'hai, Donna benchè di rado et con riguardo, Perché donna non vuoi, Quando lo pomo vien da lo pomaro, Son io donna qual mostri ogni tuo bene). Musicologically: the Italian-born composers in SIMSSA are Palestrina (sacred mass) and the anonymous/known composers of 16th-century Italian madrigals. Note: wdt:P27 (citizenship) = Q38 returns 0 results because Italy as a modern sovereign state (Q38) did not exist in the Renaissance — the correct historical birthplace test is P19 (place of birth) → P17 (country of the birthplace QID) = Q38. Q38 = Italy. New database: first federated query covering SIMSSA. Tests: 2-hop local-Person-node pattern (Work → simssa:Person → wdt:P2888 → composerQID), 2-hop Wikidata birthplace filter (P19 → P17).

nl: How many songs belong to each music genre in the University of Tennessee Song Index? Only return the number of songs.
notes: utsi:Song.wdt:P136 stores genre as a Wikidata QID URI (FILTER(isURI(?genre)) guards against any non-URI values; confirmed that all 10 genre values are QID URIs). Inner subquery aggregates distinct song counts per genre from UTSI (10 distinct genre QIDs); outer SERVICE retrieves the English rdfs:label for each genre from Wikidata. 10 rows returned, ordered by song count: popular music (18,203), art song (16,979), folk music (16,055), aria (2,590), spirituals (1,964), religious music (1,623), children's music (1,173), carol (531), patriotic music (512), anthem (259). Musicologically: the distribution reveals the UTSI repertoire as primarily American popular, art song, and folk traditions — the three pillars of the early-20th-century domestic song anthology tradition. New database: first federated query covering UTSI. Pattern: aggregation-before-SERVICE (GROUP BY + COUNT inside subquery, then label enrichment), identical structure to gt-101 (RISM) and gt-073 but applied to genre distribution rather than composer counts. Only 10 QIDs sent to Wikidata, making the SERVICE call very efficient.

nl: Find all tracks in the Weimar Jazz Database whose performer also appears as an artist in MusicBrainz.
notes: wdt:P175 on wjazzd:Track stores Wikidata performer QID URIs directly; mb:Artist uses wdt:P2888 to link to the same Wikidata QIDs. Cross-database join via shared Wikidata URI without any hardcoded QID. 328 of the Weimar Jazz DB's tracks have a performer also in MusicBrainz (e.g. Pat Metheny, Dexter Gordon, Fats Navarro, Wayne Shorter). First cross_database query covering wjazzd.

nl: Find songs in the University of Tennessee Song Index whose composer also appears as an artist in MusicBrainz.
notes: wdt:P86 on utsi:Song stores mixed values (some Wikidata QID URIs, some plain strings); FILTER(isURI(?composerQID)) selects only QID-linked composers. mb:Artist links to the same Wikidata QIDs via wdt:P2888. 27101 total matching songs (COUNT verified without LIMIT); LIMIT 100 in SPARQL means the query returns exactly 100 rows. Sample results: INTERMEZZO, JASMINENSTRAUCH. First cross_database query covering utsi.

nl: Find all works in SIMSSA DB whose composer also has compositions in DIAMM.
notes: Both SIMSSA and DIAMM use local Person nodes with wdt:P2888 linking to Wikidata. Cross-database join resolves via the shared composer QID. 74 SIMSSA works matched (e.g. Missa De beata virgine, Gaude virgo mater Christi, Regina celi) — all Renaissance sacred repertoire overlapping between the two databases. First cross_database query covering simssadb.

nl: Find all feast names that appear in both Cantus DB and Cantus Index.
notes: Both Cantus DB and Cantus Index store feast names as plain string literals on wdt:P837. Cross-database join is a direct string equality match. 779 distinct feast names shared (e.g. 'Acacii et sociorum', 'Ad Benedictus', 'Ad Missam'). First cross_database query covering cantusindex.

nl: Find all tracks in Dig That Lick whose performer also has solos in the Weimar Jazz Database.
notes: Both dtl:Track and wjazzd:Solo store performer as a Wikidata QID URI directly via wdt:P175. Cross-database join via shared performer QID URI — no intermediate entity or wdt:P2888 needed. 280 DTL tracks matched (e.g. Dinah, Hot Mallets, Swedish Suite), all jazz performers who appear in both corpora. Tests the direct-QID join pattern distinct from the local-Person-node pattern used in gt-078.

nl: Find all works in SIMSSA DB whose composer also has sources in RISM.
notes: Both SIMSSA and RISM use local Person nodes (simssa:Person, rism:Person) with wdt:P2888 linking to Wikidata QIDs. Join resolves via shared composer QID through two hops in each graph: Work -> Person -> wdt:P2888 -> QID. 27 SIMSSA works matched (e.g. Missa Papae Marcelli, Missa Aeterna Christi munera, Laetatus Sum) — Renaissance sacred polyphony with representation in both databases. First cross_database query joining simssadb with rism.

nl: Find all songs in the University of Tennessee Song Index whose composer also has compositions in DIAMM.
notes: UTSI stores composer as a mixed-type value on wdt:P86 (some Wikidata QID URIs, some plain strings); FILTER(isURI(?composerQID)) selects only the QID-linked ones. DIAMM uses a local Person node with wdt:P2888 to Wikidata. Cross-database join via shared Wikidata QID. 799 UTSI songs matched (e.g. IT WAS A LOVER AND HIS LASS, JE N'AI AUTRE RETENANCE) — songs whose composers appear in both the UTSI anthology index and DIAMM manuscript sources. Tests the asymmetric join pattern: one graph stores QID directly, the other via a local entity node.

nl: Find all MusicBrainz recordings whose credited artist also has musical sources preserved in RISM.
notes: mb:Recording.wdt:P175 links to mb:Artist; mb:Artist.wdt:P2888 contains a mix of external URLs including Wikidata QID URIs. rism:Person.wdt:P2888 stores Wikidata QID URIs. The join resolves for the subset where MB has the same Wikidata QID URI as RISM. 898 recordings matched (e.g. Götterdämmerung scenes by Wagner, Chopin preludes, Hildegard von Bingen's O vis aeternitatis, Beethoven trios) — classical composers documented in both RISM manuscript archives and MusicBrainz recordings. MB is ~8% loaded so result count is partial. Tests: two-hop Person-node join where both sides use wdt:P2888 to a shared Wikidata URI.

nl: Find all musical keys used in both Irish traditional music settings and jazz solos.
notes: Both ts:TuneSetting and wjazzd:Solo store tonality/key via wdt:P826 as Wikidata QID URIs. Cross-database join is a direct property-value match on the shared key QID — no intermediate entity node needed. 9 distinct key QIDs shared (e.g. Q5728362, Q913790, Q1022293, Q283895 — a spread of major and minor keys used in both Irish traditional music and jazz). Musicologically interesting: these are the modal/tonal common ground between two very different folk traditions. Tests the simplest possible cross-database join: direct wdt:P826 value equality.

nl: Find composers whose works appear in DIAMM, RISM, and MusicBrainz.
notes: Three-database join via shared Wikidata QID: diamm:Person.wdt:P2888, rism:Person.wdt:P2888, and mb:Artist.wdt:P2888 must all resolve to the same URI. DIAMM and RISM use a two-hop Person-node pattern; MB Artist stores the QID directly in wdt:P2888 (among other external URLs). 137 distinct composer names matched (e.g. Palestrina, Monteverdi, Heinrich Schütz, Giaches de Wert, Oswald von Wolkenstein, Leonel Power) — Renaissance and Baroque polyphonic composers with manuscripts in DIAMM, sources in RISM, and an artist entry in MusicBrainz. MB is ~8% loaded so some composers may be absent. Tests: 3-way cross-graph join using the same shared-QID mechanism across three databases simultaneously.

nl: Find all archives in DIAMM that are also documented as institutions in RISM.
notes: diamm:Archive.wdt:P2888 and rism:Institution.wdt:P2888 both store Wikidata institution QID URIs. Cross-database join resolves via the shared QID: 337 distinct DIAMM archives are also documented as RISM institutions (e.g. Alamire Foundation, Archives Départementales du Nord, East Kent Archives Centre — European archives holding both medieval music manuscripts catalogued in DIAMM and sources catalogued in RISM). This is the first cross_database join on institution identity (rather than person/composer identity). Tests: wdt:P2888 equality join between Archive and Institution entity types across two databases.

nl: Find all countries that have both folk music sessions in The Session and documented musical cultures in the Global Jukebox.
notes: ts:Session.wdt:P17 and gj:Culture.wdt:P17 both store country as Wikidata QID URIs (or occasionally a plain string literal such as 'Puerto Rico'). Cross-database join is a direct property-value equality on wdt:P17. 52 distinct country values shared (e.g. wd:Q142 France, wd:Q183 Germany, wd:Q17 Japan — countries with both a living folk session tradition and an ethnomusicologically documented indigenous culture in the Global Jukebox). Tests: direct wdt:P17 equality join between two geographically indexed databases with different scope (folk session recordings vs world music ethnography).

nl: Find all instruments that appear in both solo recordings in Dig That Lick and songs in the Global Jukebox.
notes: dtl:Solo.wdt:P870 and gj:Song.wdt:P870 both store instrumentation as Wikidata QID URIs directly. Cross-database join is a direct property-value equality on wdt:P870. 10 distinct instrument QIDs shared (including Q11405 flute, Q8350 saxophone, and 8 others) — instruments that bridge the jazz solo repertoire (Dig That Lick) and the Global Jukebox world music corpus. Musicologically interesting: identifies the organological common ground between two corpora with very different geographic and stylistic scope. Tests: direct wdt:P870 equality join between two single-hop instrumentation properties.

nl: Find all songs in UTSI whose composer also has works catalogued in RISM.
notes: utsi:Song.wdt:P86 stores mixed values (some Wikidata QID URIs, some plain strings); FILTER(isURI(?composerQID)) selects only QID-linked composers. rism:Person.wdt:P2888 stores Wikidata QID URIs. Cross-database join via the shared composer QID: 14548 total UTSI songs matched (COUNT verified); LIMIT 100 added to the NLQ because the full result set is large. Sample results include French art songs (e.g. INVITATION AU VOYAGE by Duparc, Q364983) and operetta repertoire (e.g. INDIAN LOVE CALL by Friml) — composers whose output spans both the UTSI anthology index and RISM historical archive. Tests the asymmetric join pattern: one graph stores composer QID directly on the song (utsi P86 direct URI), the other resolves via a local Person node (rism:Person→wdt:P2888). Contrast with gt-081 (simssadb+rism) which uses a two-hop local Person node on both sides.

nl: Find all compositions in the Weimar Jazz Database whose composer also has works catalogued in RISM.
notes: wjazzd:Composition.wdt:P86 stores composer as a Wikidata QID URI directly (unlike wjazzd:Solo.wdt:P175 which stores performer QID). rism:Person.wdt:P2888 stores Wikidata QID URIs. FILTER(isURI(?composerQID)) guards against any non-URI P86 values. 25 Weimar Jazz DB compositions matched, including works by Irving Berlin (Q128746: How Deep Is the Ocean, Remember), Cole Porter (Q215120: Begin the Beguine, I've Got You Under My Skin), Tom Jobim (Q200131: Desafinado, Insensatez), and Lil Hardin Armstrong (Q462562: Hotter than That — note: Q462562 maps to Lil Hardin Armstrong, Louis Armstrong's wife; Louis Armstrong's correct QID is Q1779. If wjazzd stores Q462562 as the composer of Hotter than That, that is a data error in the underlying Weimar Jazz DB). Musicologically interesting: these are 20th-century jazz composers and Tin Pan Alley songwriters whose sheet music or manuscripts are also catalogued in RISM, documenting the overlap between the jazz repertoire and historical music source archives. Tests: direct-QID join from wjazzd:Composition (no local Person node) to rism:Person node pattern.

nl: What compositions in the Weimar Jazz Database share a title with a track in Dig That Lick?
notes: Cross-database join via rdfs:label string equality — no QID or wdt:P2888 involved. Both wjazzd:Composition and dtl:Track have rdfs:label title literals; matching on the same string identifies shared repertoire. 17 distinct titles: Avalon, Basin Street Blues, Caravan, Cherokee, Dinah, Don't Blame Me, Donna Lee, I Didn't Know What Time It Was, Jo-Jo, Just Friends, Limehouse Blues, Milestones, Nutty, St. Thomas, Summertime, Undecided, Yesterdays. All are canonical jazz standards documented in both the Weimar Jazz Database (which has transcribed and analyzed solos) and Dig That Lick (which indexes a separate set of jazz solos). Musicologically interesting: identifies the shared repertoire core between two independent jazz corpora assembled by different research teams. Tests the label-string equality join pattern — distinct from all wdt:P2888 and direct-QID join patterns used in other cross_database queries.

nl: Find composers whose works are encoded in SIMSSA and who are also catalogued as artists in MusicBrainz.
notes: simssa:Work.wdt:P86 → simssa:Person.wdt:P2888 matches mb:Artist.wdt:P2888 — both store the same Wikidata QID. Two-hop join on the simssadb side (local Person node → QID), single-hop on the MusicBrainz side. 15 distinct composer name strings returned: all Renaissance polyphonic composers (Josquin des Prez, Palestrina, Ockeghem, Isaac, Obrecht, Busnoys, La Rue, Compère, etc.) whose works are both encoded in SIMSSA (digital scores for computational music analysis) and represented in MusicBrainz recordings. Note: 'des Prez Josquin' appears as a second rdfs:label variant for Josquin in SIMSSA — a data quirk, not a separate composer. Tests the two-hop wdt:P2888 identity join from simssa:Person to mb:Artist; same join mechanism as simssadb+diamm (gt-078) and simssadb+rism (gt-081) but with MusicBrainz as the second hub.

nl: Find musical modes that appear in both Cantus DB chants and Weimar Jazz Database solos.
notes: cdb:Chant.wdt:P826 and wjazzd:Solo.wdt:P826 both store tonality/mode as Wikidata QID URIs — direct property-value equality join on wdt:P826, no intermediate node needed. 3 shared mode QIDs returned: Q283895 (G minor), Q283903 (D minor), Q309994 (C minor) — modal identities that bridge 9th–16th century Catholic plainchant (Cantus DB) and 20th-century jazz improvisation (Weimar Jazz DB). Musicologically interesting: reveals which modal frameworks appear in both the medieval sacred music tradition and modern jazz practice. Tests the same single-hop P826 equality pattern as gt-084 (thesession+wjazzd) but applied to a historically much more distant database pair.

nl: Find all compositions in the Weimar Jazz Database whose composer also has songs documented in UTSI.
notes: utsi:Song.wdt:P86 stores composer as a mixed value (Wikidata QID URIs or plain strings); FILTER(isURI(?composerQID)) retains only QID-linked composers. wjazzd:Composition.wdt:P86 stores composer as a Wikidata QID URI directly. Cross-database join via direct QID equality on wdt:P86 — no local Person node on either side (contrast with simssadb+diamm gt-078 which navigates through local Person nodes). 70 distinct jazz composition labels, including April in Paris, Begin the Beguine, Caravan, Cherokee, Desafinado, Don't Get Around Much Anymore — all jazz standards by composers (Cole Porter, Duke Ellington, Thelonious Monk, Tom Jobim, etc.) who also have songs documented in the UTSI anthology. 31 distinct composers matched; 1551 UTSI songs satisfy the join on their side. Musicologically interesting: quantifies the overlap between the Tin Pan Alley / popular song tradition (UTSI) and the jazz compositional canon (Weimar Jazz DB).

nl: Find instruments that appear in solos in both Dig That Lick and the Weimar Jazz Database, and also feature in songs in the Global Jukebox.
notes: Three-database intersection via direct wdt:P870 (instrumentation) equality: dtl:Solo.wdt:P870, wjazzd:Solo.wdt:P870, and gj:Song.wdt:P870 all store Wikidata instrument QID URIs directly. 6 instrument QIDs in the triple intersection (including Q8350 saxophone and 5 others), a tighter subset of the 10 found in gt-088 (dtl+gj) and 9 found in the wjazzd+gj pair. Instruments that fall out of the 3-way intersection include Q5994 (piano), Q6607 (guitar), and Q8343 (clarinet) — present in wjazzd+gj but absent from dtl, revealing the narrower organological core shared across all three corpora. Musicologically interesting: the 6 instruments represent the universal cross-cultural toolkit appearing in both independent jazz solo corpora (Dig That Lick and Weimar Jazz DB) and the Global Jukebox ethnomusicological corpus. Tests: 3-graph direct-QID equality join on wdt:P870, a new 3-database combination extending the 2-way P870 join pattern established in gt-088.

nl: What countries have both traditional music sessions in The Session and popular songs documented in UTSI?
notes: ts:Session.wdt:P17 stores country as a Wikidata QID URI (FILTER(isURI(?country)) guards against the small number of plain-string country literals in The Session). utsi:Song.wdt:P1071 (location of creation) stores country/city as a Wikidata QID URI. Cross-database join via direct property-value equality on the shared country QID: 50 distinct country Wikidata URIs matched (including Q142 France, Q17 Japan, Q183 Germany, Q16 Canada, Q20 Norway) — countries where traditional folk music sessions occur AND where popular songs in the UTSI anthology were created. New pair: first cross_database query joining thesession with utsi. New mechanism: joins wdt:P17 (country) with wdt:P1071 (location of creation), distinct from all prior P826 (mode/key), P870 (instrument), P175 (performer), and P86 (composer) join patterns used elsewhere. Musicologically interesting: reveals the geographic overlap between the living Irish/folk session tradition and the popular song anthology repertoire.

nl: Find all works encoded in SIMSSA whose composer is also documented in both DIAMM and RISM.
notes: Three-database join via shared composer Wikidata QID: simssa:Person.wdt:P2888, rism:Person.wdt:P2888, and diamm:Person.wdt:P2888 must all resolve to the same URI. All three databases use the two-hop local-Person-node pattern (Work/Source → Person → wdt:P2888 → QID). 27 SIMSSA works returned (Missa Papae Marcelli, Missa Aeterna Christi munera, Missa Hodie Christus Natus Est, Laetatus Sum, Vidi Aquam, O Magnum Mysterium, Beati Immaculati, and others) — Renaissance sacred polyphony by composers (Palestrina, Victoria, Morales, etc.) with manuscripts in DIAMM, archival sources in RISM, and digital scores in SIMSSA. Notably, the 27-work count matches the 2-way simssadb+rism result (gt-081), meaning every SIMSSA composer documented in RISM also appears in DIAMM: the two historical archives cover the same Renaissance polyphonic canon completely for this small digital-score corpus. New 3-database combination: simssadb+rism+diamm not previously combined (distinct from gt-085 which uses diamm+rism+musicbrainz). Tests: 3-graph symmetric two-hop P2888 identity join pattern.

nl: Find all archives in DIAMM that also hold plainchant manuscript sources documented in Cantus DB.
notes: cdb:Source.wdt:P276 stores location as a string literal containing the institution name and RISM siglum in parentheses, e.g. 'Bayerische Staatsbibliothek (D-Mbs)'. diamm:Archive.wdt:P11550 stores the RISM siglum as a plain string, e.g. 'D-Mbs'. Cross-database join via string containment: CONTAINS(STR(?locStr), CONCAT('(', STR(?siglum), ')')) tests whether the RISM siglum embedded in the CantusDB P276 string matches a DIAMM archive's wdt:P11550 siglum. 51 distinct archive labels matched (54 distinct archive IRIs; 3 archives share a label with another archive) — major European institutions including Abbaye Saint-Pierre de Solesmes (F-SO), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana (V-CVbav), Bayerische Staatsbibliothek (D-Mbs), Biblioteca Medicea-Laurenziana (I-Fl) — libraries known for both medieval plainchant collections (CantusDB) and broader historical music manuscript holdings (DIAMM). New pair: cantusdb + diamm. New join mechanism: string-containment match across two properties of different types (P276 embedded string vs P11550 siglum literal) — the first cross_database query that joins on an embedded string identifier rather than a shared Wikidata QID or direct property-value equality. CantusDB has 235 sources; DIAMM has 681 archives with siglums.

nl: Find all countries that have medieval music manuscript archives in DIAMM and traditional music sessions in The Session.
notes: diamm:Archive.wdt:P131 links to a diamm:City node; diamm:City.wdt:P17 links to a diamm:Country node; diamm:Country.wdt:P2888 provides the Wikidata country QID (all 680 DIAMM cities have a direct wdt:P17 → Country link — only 11 cities have an intermediate Region node, making the 3-hop Archive→City→Region→Country path near-empty). ts:Session.wdt:P17 stores country as a Wikidata QID URI directly; FILTER(isURI(?countryQID)) guards against the handful of plain-string country literals in The Session. Cross-database join via shared country QID: diamm:Country.wdt:P2888 = ts:Session.wdt:P17. 28 distinct country labels matched — European countries with medieval manuscript archives (France, Germany, Italy, England, Ireland, Spain, Belgium, Netherlands, Poland, Austria, Switzerland, Portugal, Russia, Denmark, Sweden, Czech Republic, Hungary, Latvia, Iceland) plus non-European countries with active folk session communities (USA, Australia, South Africa, Peru, Colombia, Mexico). New pair: diamm + thesession. New join mechanism: 2-hop geographic path (Archive→City.wdt:P17→Country.wdt:P2888) meeting a direct P17 country QID in The Session — distinct from the single-hop P17 joins in gt-087 (thesession+gj) and gt-096 (thesession+utsi). Musicologically interesting: reveals geographic continuity between the medieval European manuscript tradition and the modern folk/traditional music session movement.

nl: Find all tracks in Dig That Lick whose performer is also catalogued as a person in RISM.
notes: dtl:Solo.wdt:P175 stores performer as a Wikidata QID URI directly. rism:Person.wdt:P2888 stores Wikidata QID URI for RISM-catalogued musicians. Cross-database join via shared performer/person QID: dtl:Solo.wdt:P175 = rism:Person.wdt:P2888. 7 distinct performers matched: Louis Armstrong (Q1779), Benny Goodman (Q46755), Lionel Hampton (Q313525), Billie Holiday (Q104358), Irving Mills (Q1292110), Artie Shaw (Q320065), Miles Davis (Q93341) — swing-era and bebop musicians whose compositions or arrangements were also formally catalogued in RISM. 27 distinct track labels returned across those 7 performers (e.g. Airmail Special, Body And Soul, Fine And Mellow, Hotter Than That, Digga Digga Do, Dinah, Darn That Dream). Musicologically interesting: identifies jazz artists who bridge both the improvised solo tradition (Dig That Lick) and the written/published music tradition documented in RISM — Irving Mills appears as a publisher who formally registered music in RISM; others appear because their arrangements were published. New pair: dig-that-lick + rism. New join mechanism: dtl:Solo.wdt:P175 (direct performer QID on Solo entity) = rism:Person.wdt:P2888 (QID on RISM Person node) — performer identity linking a jazz improvisation corpus to a historical manuscript archive.
